title: Bone and Joint Health. Grape Consumption Improves Joint Mobility and Relieves Pain Associated with Knee Osteoarthritis. A 16-week clinical study investigated the benefits of grape consumption on inflammation and osteoarthritis outcomes. A group of 72 men and women with knee osteoarthritis were assigned to consume either the equivalent of 1.5 cups of grapes or a placebo, each day. Both men and women consuming the grape-enriched diet had a significant decrease in activity-related pain from beginning to end compared to placebo and, by mid-term, an overall decrease in total knee symptoms, with this beneficial effect more pronounced in females. Age-related differences were observed related to activity: grape-consuming subjects under 64 years of age reported a 70 percent increase in very hard activity, while those receiving the placebo reported a significant decrease in very hard activity. Participants over 65 years reported a decline in moderate to hard activities whether consuming grapes or the placebo. No difference in range of motion was observed for either the grape group or the placebo group. A serum marker for inflammation measured was increased in both placebo and grape groups. However, the increase was reduced in the grape group, suggesting some modulatory influence of the grape polyphenols.

A 16-week clinical study investigated the benefits of grape consumption on inflammation and osteoarthritis outcomes.
A group of 72 men and women with knee osteoarthritis were assigned to consume either the equivalent of 1.5 cups of grapes or a placebo, each day.

Both men and women consuming the grape-enriched diet had a significant decrease in activity-related pain from beginning to end compared to placebo and, by mid-term, an overall decrease in total knee symptoms, with this beneficial effect more pronounced in females.

Age-related differences were observed related to activity: grape-consuming subjects under 64 years of age reported a 70 percent increase in very hard activity, while those receiving the placebo reported a significant decrease in very hard activity.
Participants over 65 years reported a decline in moderate to hard activities whether consuming grapes or the placebo.
No difference in range of motion was observed for either the grape group or the placebo group.
